title: English for AI
text:
1 Welcome
Welcome to English for AI: ESL-Oriented AI Fundamentals.
This course helps English learners use AI tools in safe, clear, and useful ways. You will practice English while you learn how to write prompts, compare choices, check sources, describe visuals, and explain your thinking.
AI can help you think and communicate. AI does not make the final decision for you.

Your toolkit will include prompts, vocabulary, sentence frames, decision matrices, source-checking work, a visual description, a speaking script, a safety checklist, and a final reflection.

title: English for AI
text:
1.3 Course Promise
In this course:

You write in English.
You use simple, clear prompts.
You protect private information.
You check important answers.
You make the final decision.







WarningPrivacy Rule



Do not put private information into an AI tool. Do not upload private documents. Use safe examples and public information for class practice.
crumbs: 1  English for AI

title: 2  Orientation
text:
2.1 Course Purpose
This course teaches AI as an English communication and decision-support skill. It is not a programming course. You will use AI to practice English, organize ideas, compare options, read sources, and prepare short communication tasks.
crumbs: 2  Orientation

title: 2  Orientation
text:
2.2 Who This Course Is For
This course is for English language learners with almost no previous AI knowledge. Beginner and low-intermediate students can use the sentence frames and templates. Higher-level students can write longer answers and add more detail.
crumbs: 2  Orientation

title: 2  Orientation
text:
2.3 English-First Work
Your final course work should be in English.
You may use your first language to understand instructions when your teacher allows it. But your portfolio files, prompts, answers, reflections, and presentation should be in simple English.






flowchart LR
  O[Orientation] --> C[Class lesson]
  C --> S[Self-practice]
  S --> P[Portfolio artifact]
  P --> T[English AI Decision Toolkit]




Figure 2.1: Your path through the English for AI course.





In simple English: each class and self-practice task helps you build one part of your final toolkit.

Students need only a basic device with a browser. A low-end Chromebook is enough.
Teachers may provide:

title: 2  Orientation
text:
2.5 If You Need Support
This course should feel safe and useful. If something is hard, you have options. Tell your teacher early — they can help.

You can ask for a printed copy of any page.
You can ask for more time to finish a task or save a file.
You can write some answers instead of saying them, if your teacher allows it.
You can say some answers instead of writing them, if your teacher allows it.
You do not need to record your voice unless your teacher says it is allowed.
You can use your first language to think, but please write final work in English.
Tell your teacher if a tool, link, or activity does not work on your device.
Never share private information to complete an activity. Ask for a safer example.

Asking for help is part of learning. You are not behind for asking.

title: 2  Orientation
text:
2.10 Colab AI in the Labs
Some Optional Colab Labs use Colab AI. Colab AI is Google Colab’s built-in AI helper. When you open a lab and the AI cell runs, the lab tries to use Colab AI automatically. You do not need to turn it on.
If Colab AI is available, you will see a short answer in the lab. Read it carefully and decide what to keep, change, or reject. The AI answer is for review only.
If Colab AI is not available — for example, if your account or Colab runtime does not support it — you will see a short, friendly message. The regular lab steps will still work, and you can finish your portfolio without AI.
Safety rules for every Colab AI cell:

Use safe examples only.
Never type private information into the lab.
Check important AI answers with a real source.
AI may be wrong. You decide what is true.
You make the final decision.


Available Colab AI models can change over time. The lab will pick a working model if one is available, and continue without AI if none is available.

title: 3  Class 1: AI Basics and English Prompts
text:
3.1 Start Here
Duration: 2 hours
You need help studying. You open an AI tool and type:
The answer is long, general, and not very useful. It is mostly a guess about what you need.
Now you try again with a clearer English prompt:
This time the answer is clear and useful. The only thing that changed was the prompt.

title: 3  Class 1: AI Basics and English Prompts
text:
3.2 Why This Matters
When you ask AI in clear English, you get better answers. You also practice English you can use every day — at school, at work, and with family. A good prompt is a skill. Clear English is a skill. Today you will start to build both.
crumbs: 3  Class 1: AI Basics and English Prompts

title: 3  Class 1: AI Basics and English Prompts
text:
3.3 Today You Will
By the end of this lesson, you will:

Learn the ZONI CLEAR prompt scaffold in simple English.
Compare a weak prompt with a strong prompt.

Practice a safe privacy rule: never share private information with AI.
Save: English AI Decision Toolkit/Class 1 - My First English AI Prompt.md.







NoteLesson Reference





This is the curriculum focus for today. Open this box to see the full lesson plan. You do not need to memorize it — use it as a quick guide.
Theme: AI can help when we ask clear questions in English.
AI Focus: Prompt writing.
ESL Focus: Simple requests, question words, imperatives, and polite language.
Content Objective: Students will write a clear English prompt for a simple everyday task.

crumbs: 3  Class 1: AI Basics and English Prompts

title: 3  Class 1: AI Basics and English Prompts
text:
3.4 Beginner Explanation
AI tools answer instructions called prompts. A prompt is a question or an instruction you write in English. A good prompt tells the AI what you need, gives short context, and explains the format you want for the answer.
For English learners, writing prompts is also English practice. You practice giving clear instructions, asking questions, and improving sentences that are not clear. The more clear your English, the more useful the AI answer.






WarningPrivacy Rule



Do not share private information with AI. Do not type passwords, addresses, ID numbers, medical records, immigration documents, or private school/work documents into an AI tool.
crumbs: 3  Class 1: AI Basics and English Prompts

text:
3.8 Guided Practice

3.8.1 Warm-Up: What Can AI Help With?
Sort each task.



Task
Good for AI
Use Carefully
Do Not Use AI Alone




Brainstorm lunch ideas
X




Ask for a medical diagnosis


X


Summarize a public article
X




Upload immigration papers


X


Practice interview answers
X





Pair speaking frames:
I think this is good for AI because __________.
I think we should be careful because __________.
I think we should not use AI for this because __________.


3.8.2 Mini-Lesson: The ZONI CLEAR Prompt Scaffold
This course uses a ZONI CLEAR scaffold to help you write prompts in simple English. CLEAR means Context, Limits, Example or format, Ask, and Review.



Letter
Meaning
Student-Friendly Question




C
Context
What is my situation?


L
Limit
What should AI avoid?


E
Example
What style or format do I want?


A
Ask
What do I want AI to do?


R
Review
What should AI check before answering?









flowchart LR
  C[Context] --> L[Limits]
  L --> E[Example or format]
  E --> A[Ask]
  A --> R[Review]
  R --> P[Better prompt]




Figure 3.1: The ZONI CLEAR prompt flow.





In simple English: a clear prompt gives AI the situation, the rules, the format, the task, and a check.
Student template:
I need help with: [task]

Context:
[short background]

Limits:
- Use simple English.
- Do not invent facts.
- Do not use private information.
- Ask questions if information is missing.

Output format:
[table / checklist / email / short paragraph]

Before answering:
List assumptions and missing information.


3.8.3 Weak Prompt vs Strong Prompt
Weak prompt:
Help me study.
Strong prompt:
I need help making a simple study plan.

Context:
- I have an English test on Friday.
- I can study 30 minutes each day.
- I need help with vocabulary and speaking.

Please give me:
1. A 4-day study plan.
2. A list of 10 words to review.
3. A 5-minute speaking practice activity.
4. Questions you need before improving the plan.

Use simple English.
Underline:

the task
the context
the limits
the output format

title: 5  Class 2: Making Better Decisions in English
text:
5.1 Start Here
Duration: 2 hours
A friend asks you a simple question:
Think about it. There is no single best plan for everyone.
The “best” choice changes for each person. Before you choose anything — a phone plan, a job, a class, a bus or train — you need to know what matters most to you.
crumbs: 5  Class 2: Making Better Decisions in English

title: 5  Class 2: Making Better Decisions in English
text:
What is the best phone plan?


A student who calls family abroad needs international minutes.
A delivery driver needs a lot of mobile data on the road.
A parent at home might prefer a family plan with shared data.
crumbs: 5  Class 2: Making Better Decisions in English

title: 5  Class 2: Making Better Decisions in English
text:
5.2 Why This Matters
You make decisions every day. Which job to apply for. Which class to take. How to spend your time and your money. AI can help you see your options and compare them in English. But AI does not know your life. You do. Today you will learn a simple tool to compare choices in clear English — and remember that the final decision is yours.
crumbs: 5  Class 2: Making Better Decisions in English

title: 5  Class 2: Making Better Decisions in English
text:
5.3 Today You Will
By the end of this lesson, you will:

Learn how to build a simple decision matrix in English.
Compare two real-life options using clear comparison phrases.
Practice English like “A is cheaper than B” and “I might choose A because…”
Decide why the final decision must stay with a person, not the AI.
Save: English AI Decision Toolkit/Class 2 - English Decision Matrix.md.







NoteLesson Reference





This is the curriculum focus for today. Open this box to see the full lesson plan. You do not need to memorize it — use it as a quick guide.
Theme: AI can help compare choices, but people make the final decision.
AI Focus: Decision matrix.
ESL Focus: Comparatives, superlatives, modals, and reasons.
Content Objective: Students will compare two or three options using a decision matrix.
Language Objective: Students will use English comparison language:
Option A is cheaper than Option B.
Option B is more convenient.
I should choose...
I might choose...
I chose this because...
The risk is...
crumbs: 5  Class 2: Making Better Decisions in English

title: 5  Class 2: Making Better Decisions in English
text:
5.4 Beginner Explanation
A decision matrix is a simple table for comparing choices. You pick the things that matter to you (criteria), give each option a score, and write short reasons in English. AI can help you find criteria you forgot or risks you did not see. AI should not choose for you — you make the final decision.






NoteMethod Note



This activity adapts the decision matrix, also called a Pugh matrix, decision grid, or selection matrix. A decision matrix helps compare options against criteria and can use weights when some criteria matter more (American Society for Quality n.d.).








ImportantHuman Decision Rule



AI can help you compare. You make the final decision.








flowchart LR
  O[Options] --> C[Criteria]
  C --> M[Matrix scores]
  M --> A[AI suggestions]
  A --> V[Verify facts]
  V --> D[Student decides]




Figure 5.1: AI can help compare, but the student makes the decision.





In simple English: AI can give ideas, but you check the facts and make the final choice.
crumbs: 5  Class 2: Making Better Decisions in English

title: 5  Class 2: Making Better Decisions in English
text:
5.5 Key Vocabulary








Word
Simple Meaning
Example Sentence




option
A choice
Bus and train are two options.


compare

title: 7  Class 3: Source-Grounded Reading
text:
7.1 Start Here
Duration: 2 hours
A friend asks you: “Can I return this item to the store?”
You see two answers.
Answer A:
Answer B:
Which answer do you trust more?
Answer B shows where the information came from. You can check it. Answer A might be true — but it might be wrong, and you have no way to know. Trusting an answer without a source can cost you time, money, or even a job.

title: 7  Class 3: Source-Grounded Reading
text:
7.2 Why This Matters
AI sometimes invents answers that sound correct but are not true. We call this a hallucination. If you ask AI about school rules, store policies, public notices, work guides, or health information, a wrong answer can cause real problems. Today you will learn how to give AI a short source text and ask for answers only from that source. This is a safer way to use AI, and it is excellent English reading practice.
crumbs: 7  Class 3: Source-Grounded Reading

title: 7  Class 3: Source-Grounded Reading
text:
7.3 Today You Will
By the end of this lesson, you will:

Learn how to write a source-grounded prompt.
Quote a short public text and paraphrase it in your own simple English.
Practice saying “I cannot answer from the provided source” when the answer is not there.
Spot a hallucination by checking the source.
Save: English AI Decision Toolkit/Class 3 - Source-Grounded Answer in English.md.







NoteLesson Reference





This is the curriculum focus for today. Open this box to see the full lesson plan. You do not need to memorize it — use it as a quick guide.
Theme: AI answers must be checked with sources.
AI Focus: Source-grounded answers and hallucination awareness.
ESL Focus: Reading for details, paraphrasing, evidence language, and citations.
Content Objective: Students will answer a question using only a provided source text.
Language Objective: Students will use evidence phrases:
The source says...
According to the text...
This means...
The answer is not in the source.
I need to check...
crumbs: 7  Class 3: Source-Grounded Reading

title: 7  Class 3: Source-Grounded Reading
text:
7.4 Beginner Explanation
Sometimes AI gives an answer that sounds correct but is not really supported. We call this a hallucination. The words look fine, but the facts may be invented.
A safer way to use AI for reading: give it a short source text and ask it to answer only from that text. If the answer is not in the text, AI should say so — and so should you.
In simple English:
Do not guess. Use the source.






NoteResearch Note



This classroom routine is informed by work on AI factuality, hallucination, and retrieval/source-grounded generation. In this class, we practice a simple version: use the provided source, quote short evidence, and do not guess when the source does not answer (Maynez et al. 2020; Lewis et al. 2020).

A note that shows where information came from

evidence
Words from the source that support an answer

quote
Exact words from a source

paraphrase
Same idea in your own words
I paraphrased the rule.


hallucination
An AI answer that may be wrong or unsupported
The AI hallucinated a date.

[student question]

flowchart LR

  S --> E[Find evidence]
  E --> C{Answer there?}
  C -->|Yes| Q2[Short quote]
  Q2 --> P[Paraphrase]
  C -->|No| N[Do not guess]




Figure 7.1: A safe routine for source-grounded answers.





In simple English: use the source. If the source does not answer, say so.

For important answers, stop, check the source, look for better coverage, and trace the claim back to evidence (Caulfield 2019).

title: 9  Class 4: Visual, Speaking, and Audio Communication

9.4 Beginner Explanation
A visual is a picture, flyer, or simple diagram. A clear visual helps people understand your message quickly. A script is the words you prepare before you speak. A short script helps you speak more clearly and stay calm.
AI can help you check or improve a visual description or a speaking script. You still need to check that the message is true, safe, respectful, and uses only images and voices you have permission to use.

9.8.1 Warm-Up: Clear or Confusing Communication?

Speaking frames:

9.8.2 Mini-Lesson: Safe Visuals, Open Licenses, and Consent
Students learn:

Do not copy random images without permission.
Do not use another person’s face or voice without permission.
Do not create misleading images.
Use open-license media when possible.
Course images provided in this book are ZONI® assets unless otherwise noted.
Save images in open formats like .svg or .png.
Record only your own voice if recording is allowed.
Do not imitate or clone another real person’s voice.

  P --> D[Describe in English]
  D --> S[Write script]
  S --> R[Practice]
  R --> H[Share safely]

In simple English: first check that the visual is safe to use, then describe it and practice your script.

A workflow is a process you can use again and again. Your personal English AI workflow helps you ask, check, compare, decide, and explain — in English, safely. Your final toolkit is the place where all of this lives: your prompts, your decisions, your sources, your scripts, and your safety rules. It shows your English growth and your safe AI habits.

12.8.2 Mini-Lesson: Personal AI Safety Checklist
Copy and adapt:
# Personal AI Safety Checklist

Before I use AI, I ask:

1. Is this task safe for AI?
2. Am I sharing private information?
3. Do I need a source?
4. Could this affect money, health, legal status, school, or work?
5. What would happen if the AI is wrong?
6. How will I verify the answer?
7. What final decision is still mine?
8. Can I explain my answer in English?


12.8.3 Guided Practice: My AI Decision Assistant Prompt
# My AI Decision Assistant Prompt

You are helping me think through a decision.
Do not make the decision for me.
Use simple English.
Ask questions if important information is missing.
Separate facts from assumptions.
Give me a decision matrix.
Tell me what I should verify before acting.

Decision:
[Write my decision]

Options:
1.
2.
3.

What matters most to me:
1.
2.
3.

Limits:
- Do not invent facts.
- Do not use private information.
- Tell me when professional advice is needed.

Output format:
1. Summary of my decision
2. Decision matrix
3. Pros and cons
4. Missing information
5. Risks
6. Next best step
7. Three useful English phrases for explaining my decision

Missing information I need to check:
1.
2.
3.

Final human decision reminder:
AI can help me compare. I make the final decision.

13.2 Core Vocabulary by Class









Course Part
Word
Simple Meaning
Example Sentence




Class 1
AI
A computer system that can create answers, text, images, or other outputs
I used AI to organize my ideas.


Class 1
prompt
The instruction or question you give AI
My prompt asks for a simple email.


Class 1
output
The answer from AI
The output was too long.


Class 1
context
Background information
I gave context about my schedule.


Class 1
revise
Improve after checking
I revised my prompt.


Class 1
private
Not for everyone to see
My address is private.


Self-Practice 1
format
The shape of the answer
I asked for a table format.


Self-Practice 1
variation
A different version
I wrote three prompt variations.


Class 2
option
A choice
Bus and train are two options.


Class 2
compare
Look at two or more things
I compare the cost and time.


Class 2
criteria
The things that matter
My criteria are cost and safety.


Class 2
risk
A possible problem
The risk is arriving late.


Class 2
evidence
Information that supports an answer
The price is evidence.


Class 2
decision
A choice after thinking
My decision is to take the bus.


Self-Practice 2
matrix
A table for comparing choices
I used a decision matrix.


Self-Practice 2
assumption
Something I think is true but need to check
My assumption is that the bus is on time.


Class 3
source
The original text or information
The source is a school policy.


Class 3
citation
A note that shows where information came from
I need a citation for this answer.


Class 3
quote
Exact words from a source
I used a short quote.


Class 3
paraphrase
The same idea in your own words
I paraphrased the rule.


Class 3
hallucination
An AI answer that may be wrong or unsupported
The AI hallucinated a date.


Self-Practice 3
supported
Backed up by a source
This answer is supported.


Self-Practice 3
unsupported
Not backed up by a source
This answer is unsupported.


Class 4
image
A picture
The image shows a classroom.


Class 4
license
Permission to use something
The image has an open license.


Class 4
attribution
Giving credit to the creator
I wrote an attribution.


Class 4
caption
Short text near an image
The caption explains the picture.


Class 4
script
Written words prepared before speaking
I wrote a script for my call.


Class 4
pronunciation
How words sound
I practiced pronunciation.


Class 4
consent
Permission
I need consent to use a voice recording.


Class 4
feedback
Advice to improve
My partner gave feedback.


Self-Practice 5
portfolio
A collection of work
My portfolio shows my learning.


Self-Practice 5
presentation
A short talk
I practiced my presentation.


Class 5
workflow
A repeatable process
My workflow helps me check AI answers.


Class 5
template
A reusable format
I saved a prompt template.


Class 5
guardrail
A safety rule
My guardrail is: do not share private data.


Class 5
reflection
Thinking about learning
My reflection explains what improved.


Class 5
final decision
The choice I make
AI helps, but I make the final decision.

16.2 High-Stakes Topic Warnings
High-stakes topics can affect health, legal status, money, safety, school, or work.
Examples:

medical decisions
legal problems
immigration
financial contracts
taxes
safety emergencies
mental health crises
school discipline
employment contracts

For these topics, AI may help you:

make a question list
learn vocabulary
organize notes
prepare for a conversation

AI should not be the final authority. Ask a qualified person.

title: 16  AI Safety and Privacy
text:
16.4 Source-Checking Routine
Use this routine for important answers:

Ask: What is the source?
Check the source title or link.
Find the sentence that supports the answer.
Use a short quote.
Paraphrase in simple English.
Ask: What is missing?
If the source does not answer, write: I cannot answer from the provided source.

title: 16  AI Safety and Privacy
section: 16.5 Consent Rules for Images and Audio
text:
16.5 Consent Rules for Images and Audio
Consent means permission.
Follow these rules:

Do not use another person’s face without permission.
Do not record another person’s voice without permission.
Do not use full names of minors.
Do not create misleading images of real people.
Do not imitate or clone another real person’s voice.
Use your own voice for practice if recording is allowed.
Use ZONI-provided, open-license, self-created, or teacher-provided visuals.

title: 16  AI Safety and Privacy
text:
16.6 Using Colab AI Safely
Some Optional Colab Labs use Google’s built-in Colab AI when it is available in the Colab runtime. Follow these rules every time you use a Colab AI cell:

Do not type private information. No real names, addresses, ID numbers, passwords, school records, medical, legal, immigration, financial, or employer documents.
Use safe examples only. Replace real details with safe, public-style content such as Student A, my teacher, the public library, or a community event.
AI may be wrong. Read every AI answer carefully and check it against a real source before you use it.
AI is a helper, not the final author. Save your real work as Markdown in your portfolio folder, not inside the AI tool.
You make the final decision. AI can suggest, compare, and simplify; AI cannot decide for you.
If Colab AI is unavailable, continue with the regular lab steps. The lab will still finish your portfolio task. Successful AI output is never required for portfolio completion.
The labs load a small helper file at start-up. The first cell of every lab loads interactives/e4a_colab.py from this repository’s public GitHub raw URL. You do not need to read this file. If it cannot load (no network, GitHub outage), the cell shows a short friendly message and the rest of the lab still runs — you can finish your portfolio using the regular worksheet steps in this book. There are no API keys and no external AI SDKs. Colab AI is reached only through Google Colab’s built-in helper, and only when it is available.


Available Colab AI models can change over time depending on account, region, runtime, or Colab limits. The lab will pick a working model if one is available.

title: 16  AI Safety and Privacy
text:
16.7 Vendor Lock-In Avoidance Guide
Vendor lock-in means your work is stuck inside one tool or platform.
To avoid lock-in:

Save important work outside the AI tool.
Keep prompts reusable.
Use open or common formats.
Avoid private uploads.
Keep a copy of your final files.
Ask: Can I export my work if this tool disappears?
Learn the workflow, not only one brand name.

title: 18  References and Learning Sources
text:
18.1 Course Method Map
This page shows where some course methods come from. References help us learn how to give credit. They also remind us that good classroom tools are often adapted from work by many people.
You do not need to memorize these sources. Use them as a map when you want to learn more, check a method, or model good attribution.
crumbs: 18  References and Learning Sources

title: 18  References and Learning Sources
text:
Course idea
Learning source




ZONI CLEAR prompt scaffold
This course uses a beginner English scaffold. It is different from Leo S. Lo’s CLEAR framework for prompt engineering (Lo 2023).


Decision matrix
The Class 2 activity adapts the decision matrix, also called a Pugh matrix, decision grid, or selection matrix (American Society for Quality n.d.).


Source-grounded answers
Class 3 uses a simple classroom routine informed by research on hallucination, factuality, and retrieval-augmented generation (Maynez et al. 2020; Lewis et al. 2020).


Source checking
The course’s “stop and check” habit connects to Mike Caulfield’s web literacy and SIFT work (Caulfield 2017, 2019).


Visual attribution
Class 4 uses Creative Commons attribution practices, especially TASL: Title, Author, Source, License (Creative Commons n.d.).


ESL scaffolding
Content objectives, language objectives, sentence frames, vocabulary support, and sheltered instruction routines connect to SIOP-style teaching (Echevarria et al. 2017).


AI literacy
The course focuses on practical AI literacy: recognizing AI, using it carefully, checking output, and thinking about social impact (Long and Magerko 2020).


Feedback tools
Course feedback tools use clear criteria and performance descriptions (Brookhart 2013).


Course design
The 5-class sequence and portfolio use backward design: start with the final evidence, then plan learning steps (Wiggins and McTighe 2005).


Responsible AI in education
Course safety guidance is informed by education and risk-management guidance (Miao and Holmes 2023; Tabassi 2023).
